coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for ratiometric detection of curcumin and ferric ion based on inner filter effect, cell imaging and PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Zu, Fanlin
Xu, Jinxia
Zhou, Xuguang
Bai, Zhangjun
Ma, Cong
Luo, Yunmei
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The CDs were used as the ratiometric probe for detection of curcumin and Fe3+. The fluorescence of CDs at 420 nm was quenched through inner filter effect (IFE) while the fluorescence of curcumin at 525 nm was enhanced after adding curcumin. The value of ln(I525/I420) increased linearly with the curcumin concentration in the range of 5–30 μM, the limit of detection was 21.79 nM. After adding Fe3+ into the system of CDs-curcumin, curcumin complexed with Fe3+ leading to the quenching of the fluorescence of curcumin at 525 nm, which declined the spectra overlap between curcumin and CDs. The process of IFE was inhibited, the fluorescence of CDs at 420 nm was recovered. The value of ln(I525/I420) linearly decreased with the increasing of Fe3+ in the range of 2-14 μM, the limit of detection is 18.11 nM. The CDs-curcumin system was successfully applied for H1299 cell imaging and has potential applications in the fields of fluorescent labeling and inhibition of tumor cells. The CDs-curcumin system was furtherly used for PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Curcumin
Fe3+
Inner filter effect
H1299 cell imaging
Membrane fouling research

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs
PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment
Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs for detection of curcumin
Mechanism of CDs for detection of curcumin
Detection of Fe3+ with the system of CDs-curcumin
Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs-curcumin for detection of Fe3+
Mechanism of CDs-curcumin for detection of Fe3+
H1299 cells imaging of CDs and curcumin
PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-resolution TEM images of CDs (inset) corresponding size distribution histogram of CDs; (B) XPS spectra of CDs;(C) FTIR spectra of CDs;(D) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s；(E) high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s；(F) high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths;(C) UV–vis absorption spectra of curcumin;(D) Fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different curcumin concentrations from 0 to 30 μM under 350 nm excitation wavelength; (B) The CIE titration curve of curcumin from 0 to 30 μM; (C) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity ln(I525/I420) versus different curcumin concentrations from 5 to 30 μM; (D) Fluorescence spectra of selective detection of curcumin.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectral of curcumin, curcumin + CDs and CDs; (B) Excitation, emission spectra of CDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of curcumin. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + curcumin; (D) Suppressed efficiency of observed (black line, Eobsd) and corrected (red line, Ecor) fluorescence intensity of CDs detecting curcumin.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs-curcumin in anhydrous ethanol with different Fe3+ concentrations; (B) The CIE titration curve of Fe3+ from 0 to 14 μM; (C) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity ln(I525/I420) versus different Fe3+ concentrations; (D) Fluorescence spectra of selective detection of Fe3+.

(A) UV-vis absorbance spectra of curcumin with different concentrations of Fe3+; (B) The Benesi–Hildebrand plot for the absorption intensity of curcumin and Fe3+ system (curcumin: Fe3+ = 2:1); (C) Job’s plot of the complex formed by [Fe3+]/ [Fe3++Curcumin]. The total concentration of Fe3+ and Curcumin was 30 μM; (D) Suppressed efficiency of observed (black line, Eobsd) and corrected (red line, Ecor) fluorescence intensity of CDs-curcumin detecting Fe3+.
Schematic illustration of CDs acting as probes for detecting curcumin and Fe3+.

(A) bright field confocal images of CDs in H1299 cells; (B) bright field confocal images of CDs + curcumin in H1299 cells; (C) bright field confocal images of the CDs + curcumin + Fe3+ in H1299 cells;(D) black field confocal images of the CDs in H1299 cells; (E) black field confocal images of the of CDs + curcumin in H1299 cells; (F) black field confocal images of CDs + curcumin + Fe3+ in H1299 cells.
Device flow chart for PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of curcumin on the fluorescence of CDs.
The IFE mechanism of CDs-curcumin with different concentrations of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for ratiometric detection of curcumin and ferric ion based on inner filter effect, cell imaging and PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology, College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin, 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology
College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Ioint International Research Loboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical University, Zunyi, 563003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Ioint International Research Loboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo, 0010024, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CDs were used as the ratiometric probe for detection of curcumin and Fe3+. The fluorescence of CDs at 420 nm was quenched through inner filter effect (IFE) while the fluorescence of curcumin at 525 nm was enhanced after adding curcumin. The value of ln(I525/I420) increased linearly with the curcumin concentration in the range of 5–30 μM, the limit of detection was 21.79 nM. After adding Fe3+ into the system of CDs-curcumin, curcumin complexed with Fe3+ leading to the quenching of the fluorescence of curcumin at 525 nm, which declined the spectra overlap between curcumin and CDs. The process of IFE was inhibited, the fluorescence of CDs at 420 nm was recovered. The value of ln(I525/I420) linearly decreased with the increasing of Fe3+ in the range of 2-14 μM, the limit of detection is 18.11 nM. The CDs-curcumin system was successfully applied for H1299 cell imaging and has potential applications in the fields of fluorescent labeling and inhibition of tumor cells. The CDs-curcumin system was furtherly used for PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as the most advanced nanoparticles have attracted many attentions in the field of analytical chemistry [1–4]. CDs show very excellent properties in terms of chemical inertness, easy surface modification, photostability and biocompatibility [5–9]. Due to these merits of CDs, it was used for detecting analytes including ions, organics and biological macromolecule [10]. The principle of CDs for detecting analytes is based on fluorescence quenching or recovery of CDs. Ratiometric fluorescent probes was constructed as fluorescent sensors with dual-emission properties using two kinds of fluorescent materials of different emission wavelengths. However, it is important to note that most of the developed probes are either based on single channel of signal “ON” or signal “OFF”, which can cause false negative or false positive results. Comparing to single-channel signal readout, the ratiometric probes can outstandingly decrease the interfering of environment and background factors by recording the ﬂuorescence intensity ratio of two diﬀ ;erent emission bands, which can improve the sensing accuracy andreliabilit [11–13]. The quenching mechanisms of CDs include static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [14,15]. Among these quenching mechanisms, IFE does not need complicated surface modification and particular distance between quenchers and CDs. Specially, when the quenchers have fluorescence emission, IFE can be utilized to design ratiometric detection method which can reduce the interference of environment around the probe [16–18]. In consideration of these advantages of IFE, it is necessary to design assays based on IFE with CDs for ratiometric detection of analytes.

Curcumin is a common natural edible pigment which can be extracted from turmeric and other rhizomes. It has anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidation and anti-tumor effects [19–21]. The absorbance spectra of curcumin show the partial overlap with the emission spectra of reported blue-emissive CDs. The property provides prerequisites for the detecting curcumin with CDs based on IFE. Meanwhile, UV absorbance about 420 nm and fluorescence emission at 525 nm of curcumin can be changed when curcumin complexes with ferric ion (Fe3+). Furthermore, the CDs-curcumin system may be promising for ratiometric detection of Fe3+.

Herein, CDs were firstly used to design the probe for ratiometric detection of curcumin and Fe3+ based on IFE. With the addition of curcumin, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by curcumin via IFE and the fluorescence of curcumin was enhanced. The CDs were used as ratiometric probe for detecting curcumin. After adding Fe3+ in the CDs-curcumin system, curcumin complexed with Fe3+, which led to the decline of overlap between the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of curcumin. The effect of IFE was inhibited, which led to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of curcumin was quenched by Fe3+. Ratiometric detection of Fe3+ can be achieved via the CDs-curcumin system. More importantly, the CDs-curcumin system was applied for H1299 cell imaging and PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), quinine sulfate (95%) and curcumin were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). EDTA-2Na, AgNO3, KCl, Pb(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, Ba(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, FeCl2, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7 2- and FeCl3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tyrosine (Tyr), tyrosine (Tyr), alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), cysteine (Cys), threonine (Thr), glutathione (GSH), doxycycline (DOX), oxytetracycline (JOX), primiamycin (PRI), roxithromycin (ROX), ofloxacin (OFL) and clarithromycin (CLA) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane was purchased from Tianjin motian membrane technology company. Deionized water and anhydrous ethanol were used throughout the experiments. All chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were used on a JEOL JEM 2011 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901) was utilized to measure absorption spectra. Fluorescence spectra were obtained by an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Gangdong scientific and technological development. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S) was used to measure XPS spectra. PVDF membrane was observed by Olympus Zeiss 710 laser scanning confocal microscopy (Germany). Cell imaging was performed using Nikon TE2000-U inverted fluorescence microscope (Japan).

CA and DETA were chosen as carbon source and nitrogen source respectively to prepare CDs. 1.3 g CA and 1.5 mL DETA were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. Then the solution was transferred to 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave, it was heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. When the reactors were cooled to ambient temperature by naturally, using rotary evaporation to remove the solvent, the obtained solid was dissolved in ethanol and the insoluble precipitate was removed by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min). The CDs were obtained by dialyzing (MW = 3500 Da) the final product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate is 0.54 at 360 nm. Quinine sulfate was used as reference and the slope method was used to determine the fluorescence quantum yield. The equation was used: φx = φst(Kx/Kst)(ηx/ηst)2, where fluorescence quantum yield is φ, K is the slope of fitting straight line, η is the refractive index of the solution, X represents the unknown sample, and st refer to the standard product.

1 mg mL-1 CDs were prepared in anhydrous ethanol at room temperature. 0.036 g curcumin was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol, moved into 100 mL capacity bottle and fixed capacity to form 1000 μM curcumin solution which was stored at room temperature. The solution of curcumin with different volumes were fixed into 0.03 mL CDs solution of 0–30 μM with curcumin concentration. The fluorescence spectra were tested under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm with 5/5 nm slit width.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in anhydrous ethanol. 0.03 mL CDs solution and 30 μM curcumin solution were taken in 10 mL volumetric bottles, the standard stock solution of Fe3+ was prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of FeCl3. Different volumes of 0.002 M Fe3+ solution of were added to form the concentration ranging from 0–14 μM solution of Fe3+. The fluorescence spectra were operated by operating the fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm (5/5 nm slit width).

MTT assay in H1299 cells was conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs and curcumin. The cells were incubated in medium of RPMI 1640 with 15% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, CDs and curcumin were respectively added in with 0-100 μg mL-1 for 24 hours. The equation was used to calculate the cell viability: cell viability (%) = the absorbance of experimental group/the absorbance of blank control group × 100%.

1 mg mL-1 CDs solution was added into the plates and incubated with H1299 cells at 37 °C for 4 h. The cells were washed three times with PBS to remove CDs out of cells. It was observed by laser scanning confocal microscopy. Then 30 μM curcumin was then added into above plates to incubate for 0.5 h at 37 °C. The cells were washed three times with PBS to remove curcumin out of cells and observed by inverted fluorescence microscope. After that, 14 μM Fe3+ solution was added in above plates to incubate for 0.5 h at 37 °C, then PBS was used to wash the Fe3+ out of cells three times, the cells were observed by inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The PVDF membrane was firstly pretreated with 1 mg mL-1 CDs solution. Specifically, the PVDF membrane was flushed evenly with 1 mg mL-1 CDs solution for 3 min. Then the PVDF membrane was placed in the device as shown in Fig.9 . Before detecting Fe3+ in the flocculant, the valve of flocculant was closed and the curcumin valve was opened. After 5 min, the PVDF membrane was taken out from the device and observed by laser scanning confocal microscopy. Then the PVDF membrane was put back on the device and the valve of curcumin was close, then the valve of the flocculant was open. After 5 min, the PVDF membrane was taken out and placed under the laser confocal microscope to observe the color of fluorescence.

The morphology of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) images as shown Fig. 1 A. It showed that the CDs were uniformly dispersed and quasi-spherical structures. The average particle diameter of CDs was estimated to be 5.2 nm as shown in Fig. 1A inset. Fig. 1A showed the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs, it displayed that the CDs had crystallinity with the lattice fringes 0.291 nm. FT-IR spectra were measured to identify the surface groups of CDs as shown in Fig. 1C. The characteristic absorption peak at 3126 cm-1 and 3133 cm-1 were assigned to N–H. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1712 cm-1, 1724 cm-1 and 1637 cm-1 were correspond to the carbonyl group. The FT-IR showed that the surface of CDs contains hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl. The XPS survey spectra were used to investigate the element composition of CDs as shown in Fig. 1D. The XPS survey spectra showed C1 s peak at about 285 eV, N1 s peak at about 400 eV and O1 s peak at about 533 eV, which illustrates the CDs contain C, N and O elements. Deconvolution of the C1 s spectra revealed two peaks at 288.1 eV and 291.9 eV, which are assigned to NCN and CC. The N 1 s spectrum peaks at 402.2 eV and 402.5 eV indicate that the N mainly existed in the manner of NH and NCO bonds. The O 1 s spectrum could be divided into two components at 532.4 eV and 533.8 eV, associated with COH and NO bands, respectively. The results of high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s are in accord with FI-RT results.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence Optical properties of CDs and curcumin emission spectra were measured to investigate optical properties of CDs and curcumin. Fig. 2 A showed the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. The CDs had two distinct absorption peaks at 260 nm and 350 nm, which are caused by the π–π* transition of CC and the n–π* transition of CO, respectively. Under the sun light, the solution of CDs is light yellow. Under the UV lamp, blue fluorescence appears as shown in the Fig. 2A. Fig. 2B showed the emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. With the increasing of excitation wavelength from 270 to 400 nm, a strong emission peak centers on 420 nm with the excitation of 350 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs reveal that the CDs process the property of the excitation wavelength-independent emission, which is attributed to the uniform surface state of CDs [10]. Fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of curcumin were shown in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D. The absorption peak of curcumin locates at 420 nm. The color of curcumin is yellow under fluorescent light, yellow-green fluorescence appears under UV light as shown in the Fig. 2C. Fig. 2D showed that the maximum fluorescence emission peak of curcumin locates at 525 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs for detection of curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence response of CDs for detecting curcumin were investigated by fluorescence spectra. Fig. 3 A showed that the change of fluorescence spectra of CDs with the concentration of curcumin varying from 0–30 μM. With the increase of the concentration of curcumin, the fluorescence of CDs at 420 nm gradually decreases, the fluorescence of curcumin at 525 nm gradually increases. The value of ln(I525/I420) is found to have a linear relationship with the concentration of curcumin from 5-30 μM as shown in Fig. 3C, the linear equation is ln(I525/I420) = 0.176x−0.897, x refers to the concentration of curcumin. The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9777. The detection limit is 21.79 nM according to LOD = 3σ/K. The CIE titration curve of curcumin was shown in Fig. 3B, which showed that the fluorescence color gradually changes from the original blue to yellow-green.

Selectivity of CDs towards curcumin was evaluated through two comparative experiments. First, 30 μM curcumin and 200 μM other organics, including Tyrosine (Tyr), tyrosine (Tyr), alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), cysteine (Cys), threonine (Thr), glutathione (GSH), doxycycline (DOX), oxytetracycline (JOX), primiamycin (PRI), roxithromycin (ROX), ofloxacin (OFL) and clarithromycin (CLA) were respectively added in CDs solution. Then, the fluorescence spectra were measured and recorded as shown in Fig. 3D. No obvious change of the fluorescent spectra was observed except for curcumin. Then, 30 μM curcumin and 200 μM above-mentioned organics were mixed. The mixtures of curcumin and organics were added in CDs solution. The value of ln(I525/I420) was calculated as shown in Fig S1. From this result, the other coexisting organics had no obvious interference towards CDs for detecting curcumin, which suggested that the CDs had a high selectivity towards curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs for detection of curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanism for curcumin was studied by UV-via absorption spectra, fluorescence decay curves and the Parker equation. Fig. 4 A showed UV-via absorption spectra of CDs, curcumin and CDs-curcumin. Compared with the absorption spectra of curcumin alone, no new absorption peak appeared after adding CDs, indicating that there was not chemical reaction between CDs and curcumin. Static quenching requires the formation of nonfluorescent ground state complex which can change the absorption spectra of CDs. So, the quenching mechanism of CDs is not static quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs without curcumin is 9.02 ns, the fluorescence lifetime after adding curcumin is 8.30 ns and the fluctuation is less than 1 ns which has no obvious change as shown in Fig. 3C. This result eliminates the dynamic quenching mechanism and FRET mechanism because the fluorescence lifetime of CDs will be reduced when the two mechanisms exist. Fig. 4B showed that the absorption of curcumin overlaps the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Based on this phenomenon, the process of FRET or IFE can occur, but the fluorescence lifetime dates exclude the FRET mechanism, so IFE was speculated as the quenching mechanism of CDs [15], in order to better verify the existence of the IFE mechanism, the Parker equation was studied [22–24].

From the Parker equation, quantitative analysis was conducted to demonstrate the IFE
(1)
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 -
10
- d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 -
10
- s
A
e m
here, Fobsd refers to measured fluorescence intensity, Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity when IFE is removed from Fobsd. Aem and Aex are the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength. s refers to the thickness of the excitation beam. g refers to the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette. d refers to the width of the cuvette. Therefore, the impact of IFE increases as the value of Fcor/Fobsd. Herein, s = 0.1 cm g = 0.4 cm d = 1.0 cm, the calculated values are shown in Table 1 , which corresponds to the UV-vis absorption of curcumin at different concentrations as shown in Figure S2. With the increase of curcumin concentration, the value of Fcor/Fobsd and the suppressed efficiency of observed increases as shown in Fig. 4D, which proves that the effect of IFE increases with the increase of curcumin concentration, further confirming the existence of IFE mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ with the system of CDs-curcumin
Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs-curcumin for detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence response of CDs-curcumin for detecting Fe3+ was investigated by fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 5 A, after adding Fe3+ to CDs- curcumin system, the fluorescence of CDs is enhanced and the fluorescence of curcumin is quenched. The value of ln(I525/I420) has a linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 5C, the linear equation is ln(I525/I420) =−0.212x+2.622, x refers to the concentration of Fe3+. The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9913. The detection limit is calculated as 18.11 nM according to LOD = 3σ/K. Fig. 5B showed CIE plot of Fe3+ titration. With the addition of Fe3+, the color of the fluorescence changes from yellowish green to blue, which is in contrast to the above-described phenomenon of changes in the fluorescence of the detecting curcumin.

Selectivity of CDs-curcumin detecting Fe3+ was investigated by the following experiments. First, CDs solution containing 30 μM curcumin was respectively added in 14 μM Fe3+ and 200 μM other cations which includes Ag+, K+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, MnO4- and Cr2O7 2-. The fluorescence spectra were recorded as shown in Fig. 5D. Then, CDs solution containing 30 μM curcumin was mixed with 14 μM Fe3+, the mixtures were respectively added in 200 μM above cations. The value of ln(I525/I420) was calculated as shown in Fig S3. The results showed that both Fe3+ and Cu2+ can cause a significant change of the value of ln(I525/I420), which limits the application of the probe. EDTA-2Na is a good chelating agent for complexing divalent ions, Fe3+ also can complex with EDTA-2Na, but the complexing constant with Fe3+ is as low as 100 orders of magnitude compared to that of Cu2+ and complexation with Fe3+ only occurs when the value of pH belongs to acidic, but the process of CDs-curcumin system detecting Fe3+ was conducted in a neutral environment. Based on this, EDTA-2Na was used to remove Cu2+ which enabled the CDs-curcumin to specifically detect Fe3+ as shown in Figure S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs-curcumin for detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of CDs-curcumin detecting Fe3+ was confirmed by UV–vis absorbance spectra, the Benesi-Hildebrand equation, Job’s plot and the Parker equation. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs-curcumin were measured with the addition of Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 6 A. The UV–vis absorbance of curcumin at 420 nm have obvious change after adding Fe3+, which illustrates that curcumin can react with Fe3+. The Benesi–Hildebrand equation was used for quantitative analysis of the reaction ratio as shown in Fig. 6B and Table S1 [25]. A linear curve between
A
m a x
-
A 0
A -
A 0
- 1 and
1
[
F e
3 +
]
0.5
is obtained in the range of 2–12 μM with a linear equation y = −11.5x + 8.3, where A0 and A are the absorption intensities of curcumin in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively as shown in Table S1. According to Benesi-Hildebrand equation, the binding ratio of curcumin and Fe3+ was concluded to be 2:1 in this system and the binding constant K was determined to be 1.07 × 105 M-1. Job’s plot was furtherly used to confirm the binding ratio of curcumin and Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 6C, which illustrates that the binding ratio of curcumin and Fe3+ is 2:1. The reaction between curcumin and Fe3+ leads to the fluorescence quenching of curcumin. Due to the decrease of absorbance of curcumin at 420 nm after the binding between curcumin and Fe3+, the overlap of the absorption spectra of curcumin and the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs decreases. It is speculated that the effect of IFE decreases with the decrease of the overlap of spectra. For conforming this speculation, the Parker equation was used in Table 2 . It is observed that the value of Fcor / Fobsd and the suppressed efficiency of observed decreases with the increase of concentration of Fe3+ as shown in Fig.6D, which indicates that impact of IFE decreases with the increase of concentration of Fe3+. The schematic illustration of CDs acting as probes for detecting curcumin and Fe3+ was shown in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: H1299 cells imaging of CDs and curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was used for investigating the cytotoxic of CDs and curcumin. From Fig. S5, it was observed that the cell viability values exceeded 75% when the concentration of CDs and curcumin reached to 0.1 mg/mL, which showed the CDs and curcumin had low cytotoxic effect on H1299 cells. For investigating the performance of bioimaging applications of CDs and curcumin, the H1299 cells imaging experiment were carried out. CDs solution was added into culture medium containing H1299 cells and incubated for 1 h. After that, the solution was washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) three times. Fig. 8 D displayed that H1299 cell emitted the blue fluorescence. Then H1299 cells were supplemented with curcumin in the growth medium for another 30 min at 37 ℃. It was observed that yellow fluorescent appeared from H1299 cell as shown in Fig. 8E, because the CDs were quenched by curcumin via IFE. 14 μM Fe3+ was added into culture medium for incubating another 30 min. The fluorescent was changed from yellow to blue, which was attributed to the react between curcumin and Fe3+ leading the recovery of the fluorescent of CDs (Fig. 8F). Since curcumin has the function of inhibiting tumor cells, based on above fluorescent phenomena, the CDs-curcumin system has potential applications in tumor cell fluorescent labeling and tumor inhibition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The PVDF membrane was firstly pretreated with 1 mg mL-1 CDs solution. Specifically, the PVDF membrane was flushed evenly with 1 mg mL-1 CDs solution for 3 minutes. Then the PVDF membrane was placed in the device as shown in Fig.9. Before detecting Fe3+ in the flocculant, the valve of flocculant was closed and the curcumin valve was opened. After 5 minutes, the PVDF membrane was taken out from the device and observed by laser scanning confocal microscopy, the yellow fluorescence was observed, the reason is that the blue fluorescence of CDs is quenched by curcumin vis IFE and the yellow fluorescence of curcumin is enhanced. Then the PVDF membrane was put back on the device and the valve of curcumin was closed, then the valve of the flocculant was open. After 5 min, the PVDF membrane was taken out and placed under the laser confocal microscope to observe the color of fluorescence. The blue fluorescence was observed under the laser confocal microscope, the reason is that the fluorescence of curcumin is quenched by Fe3+ and the IFE is inhibited, which restores the blue fluorescence of CDs. This device can be used for PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.

In summary, the CDs were firstly used for detecting curcumin and Fe3+ as a ratiometric probe in environmental and biological samples. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched while the fluorescence of curcumin was enhanced with the concentration of curcumin from 5-30 μM. This mechanism was validated as inner filter effect (IFE) through investigating the UV-via absorbance, fluorescence lifetime and the Parker equation. Upon the addition of Fe3+ in CDs-curcumin system from 2-14 μM, curcumin can complex with Fe3+ in ratio of 2:1 leading to quenching of the fluorescence of curcumin, which was confirmed by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation and Job’s plot. It inhibited the IFE and recovered the fluorescence of CDs according to the Parker equation. The CDs-curcumin system showed low toxicity and was successfully applied for H1299 cell imaging, which suggests that this system is expected to have potential applications in fluorescent labeling and inhibition of tumor cells. In addition, this system was used for PVDF membrane fouling research of iron flocculants in wastewater treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51638011, 51678409, 51508383), and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507) and the Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (No.16PTSYJC00110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.144.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
